basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: al-Insān — “Man,” also called al-Dahr, “Time” — opens with the most vertiginous of questions: was there not for man a span of time when he was not even a thing worth mentioning? Before being something, man was nothing. Qushayrī turns the meaning toward tenderness: “not an instant passed over you but you were present to My remembrance; were you ever for an instant outside My keeping? were you ever forsaken an hour of a fresh attention and an added protection?” The sūra then unfolds the portrait of the righteous (al-abrār) — those who keep their vows, who feed the poor, the orphan, and the captive for the Face of God alone, expecting neither reward nor thanks — and their paradisal recompense, described by Qushayrī as an unfolding of inner springs and drinks: the spring of camphor, the spring of ginger, Salsabīl. The summit is the most pure drink (sharāb ṭahūr) of the last verse of the Gardens: not only pure in itself, but purifying — it washes the heart of the love of all that is not God. Onto this Qushayrī grafts one of his most beautiful meditations on spiritual intoxication and its degrees, up to the servant's annihilation (istihlāk) in the Real.
themes: feeding for the Face of God

versets.ar: هَلْ أَتَىٰ عَلَى الْإِنسَانِ حِينٌ مِّنَ الدَّهْرِ لَمْ يَكُن شَيْئًا مَّذْكُورًا
versets.tr: hal atā ʿalā l-insāni ḥīnun mina l-dahri lam yakun shayʾan madhkūran
versets.trad: Has there not passed over man a span of time when he was not a thing worth mentioning?
versets.commentaire: A question that opens an abyss: shayʾan madhkūran (شَيْئًا مَذْكُورًا) — a thing worth mentioning. Before existing, man was nothing, not even a name. Qushayrī turns the verse toward sweetness: “no time came over man but he was present to My remembrance. Were you ever forsaken an hour of My keeping? were you ever, for an instant, left to yourself — My rope cast upon your back? were you ever emptied an hour of a fresh care and an added protection?” Man thinks himself forgotten; he was held in thought before ever being drawn from nothingness.

mots.racine: ح ي ن / د ه ر
ش ي أ / ذ ك ر

versets.ar: إِنَّا خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ أَمْشَاجٍ نَّبْتَلِيهِ فَجَعَلْنَاهُ سَمِيعًا بَصِيرًا
versets.tr: innā khalaqnā l-insāna min nuṭfatin amshājin nabtalīhi fa-jaʿalnāhu samīʿan baṣīran
versets.trad: Truly, We created man, to test him, from a composite fluid, and We gave him hearing and sight.
versets.commentaire: nuṭfa amshāj (نُطْفَة أَمْشاج): a drop of mixtures, glosses Qushayrī: a blend from man and woman. nabtalīhi: We test him. From this tiny mixture God makes a hearing and seeing being: He endows the dust with senses, and with the senses comes the trial — for to hear and to see is to be able to acknowledge or to refuse. The gift of faculties is already the beginning of the test.

mots.racine: س م ع / ب ص ر

versets.ar: إِنَّا هَدَيْنَاهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شَاكِرًا وَإِمَّا كَفُورًا
versets.tr: innā hadaynāhu l-sabīla immā shākiran wa-immā kafūran
versets.trad: We guided him to the path, whether he be grateful or ungrateful.
versets.commentaire: “We made known to him the way — that of good and that of evil,” glosses Qushayrī. God points out the path, then leaves man free: shākir (grateful) or kafūr (ungrateful). And Qushayrī adds the inner note: “if he gives thanks, it is by divine assistance (tawfīq); if he denies, it is by abandonment (khidhlān).” A strange balance: the way is shown to all, but the step that follows it is itself a gift. Gratitude is already a grace within the grace.

versets.ar: إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَافِرِينَ سَلَاسِلَ وَأَغْلَالًا وَسَعِيرًا
versets.tr: innā aʿtadnā li-l-kāfirīna salāsila wa-aghlālan wa-saʿīran
versets.trad: Truly, We have prepared for the deniers chains, fetters, and a blaze.
versets.commentaire: salāsil (chains), aghlāl (fetters), saʿīr (blaze). Qushayrī: “chains in which they are dragged, fetters on their necks that abase them, a raging fire.” A striking image: the denial that thought itself freedom ends in the bond. Spiritually, the real chains are the attachments one has forged for oneself; the fetter is the pride that stiffens the neck, unable to prostrate. One binds oneself before being bound.

versets.ar: إِنَّ الْأَبْرَارَ يَشْرَبُونَ مِن كَأْسٍ كَانَ مِزَاجُهَا كَافُورًا
versets.tr: inna l-abrāra yashrabūna min kaʾsin kāna mizājuhā kāfūran
versets.trad: The pure shall drink from a cup whose mixture is camphor,
versets.commentaire: al-abrār (الأَبْرار): the righteous, the good. Qushayrī gives two definitions: “the righteous one harbours no evil and harms not even an ant”; and higher: “the righteous are those whose aspiration has risen above base things, in whose hearts the springs of wisdom have welled up, who have guarded against dwelling with the world.” Their cup is mixed with camphor (kāfūr): a cool, soothing drink that quenches the fever of desires. The coolness of camphor speaks of the first state of the purified heart: rest after the fire.

versets.ar: عَيْنًا يَشْرَبُ بِهَا عِبَادُ اللَّهِ يُفَجِّرُونَهَا تَفْجِيرًا
versets.tr: ʿaynan yashrabu bihā ʿibādu llāhi yufajjirūnahā tafjīran
versets.trad: from a spring where the servants of God will drink, making it gush forth abundantly.
versets.commentaire: ʿaynan… yufajjirūnahā (عَيْنًا يُفَجِّرُونَها): a spring they make gush at will. Qushayrī gives the splendid inner reading: “even today they have in their secrets springs — the spring of love (maḥabba), the spring of clarity (ṣafāʾ), the spring of fidelity (wafāʾ), the spring of expansion (basṭ), the spring of spirit (rūḥ) — and others; and tomorrow they shall have springs.” Paradise only makes visible the fountains the heart of the righteous already carried hidden: what will gush outside was already flowing within.

mots.def: fajjara: to make gush, cause to well up.

versets.ar: يُوفُونَ بِالنَّذْرِ وَيَخَافُونَ يَوْمًا كَانَ شَرُّهُ مُسْتَطِيرًا
versets.tr: yūfūna bi-l-nadhri wa-yakhāfūna yawman kāna sharruhu mustaṭīran
versets.trad: They faithfully fulfilled their vows and feared a Day whose evil would spread far and wide.
versets.commentaire: yūfūna bi-l-nadhr (يُوفُونَ بِالنَّذْر): they keep their vows. Qushayrī: “they are faithful to the ancient pact concluded between them and God, in a manner all their own.” Faithfulness to the vow is the sign of the inner man: not an obligation imposed from outside, but the commitment the heart has taken and honours, even when no one sees it. mustaṭīr: an evil “harsh, far-reaching, spreading” — hence the watchful fear.

versets.ar: وَيُطْعِمُونَ الطَّعَامَ عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِ مِسْكِينًا وَيَتِيمًا وَأَسِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-yuṭʿimūna l-ṭaʿāma ʿalā ḥubbihi miskīnan wa-yatīman wa-asīran
versets.trad: They fed the poor, the orphan, and the captive, for the love of God, [saying:]
versets.commentaire: ʿalā ḥubbihi (عَلى حُبِّه): despite their love of food (thus in their own need), or else out of love for Him — for God, Qushayrī also says. They give to the poor, the orphan, the captive. Qushayrī recalls that the captive was then a denier — “for the Muslim did not let himself be captured in that time”: so even the defeated enemy is fed. Charity here expects nothing in return; it distinguishes neither believer nor unbeliever: it is pure overflow, like the spring of verse 6.

mots.racine: س ك ن / ي ت م / أ س ر
mots.sens: a poor man, an orphan, a captive
mots.def: miskīn: destitute; yatīm: orphan; asīr: prisoner.

versets.ar: إِنَّمَا نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ اللَّهِ لَا نُرِيدُ مِنكُمْ جَزَاءً وَلَا شُكُورًا
versets.tr: innamā nuṭʿimukum li-wajhi llāhi lā nurīdu minkum jazāʾan wa-lā shukūran
versets.trad: “We feed you only for the Face of God; we want from you neither reward nor thanks.
versets.commentaire: li-wajhi llāh (لِوَجْهِ الله): for the Face of God alone. The summit of charity: neither “reward nor thanks” expected. Qushayrī adds the decisive subtlety: “they did not say this with their tongues, but it was in their inmost hearts (ḍamāʾir).” The purest gift does not announce itself; it does not even claim the thanks, which would already be a wage. To act for the Face is to act as if no one were watching — except the One who sees all.

mots.def: wajh: face; here, the good pleasure of God Himself.

mots.racine: ج ز ي / ش ك ر

versets.ar: إِنَّا نَخَافُ مِن رَّبِّنَا يَوْمًا عَبُوسًا قَمْطَرِيرًا
versets.tr: innā nakhāfu min rabbinā yawman ʿabūsan qamṭarīran
versets.trad: Indeed, we fear from our Lord a frowning, calamitous Day.”
versets.commentaire: ʿabūs qamṭarīr (عَبُوس قَمْطَرير): a frowning, severe Day, in which faces contract — the Day of Resurrection. Here is the secret spring of their charity: not the pride of giving, but the reverent fear of a dreadful Day. They feed the hungry because they tremble before their Lord. Generosity here is born of humility: he who knows himself small before God makes himself great toward the poor.

versets.ar: فَوَقَاهُمُ اللَّهُ شَرَّ ذَٰلِكَ الْيَوْمِ وَلَقَّاهُمْ نَضْرَةً وَسُرُورًا
versets.tr: fa-waqāhumu llāhu sharra dhālika l-yawmi wa-laqqāhum naḍratan wa-surūran
versets.trad: But God has shielded them from the evil of that day, and bathed them in radiance and joy.
versets.commentaire: laqqāhum naḍratan wa-surūran (لَقّاهُمْ نَضْرَةً وَسُرورًا): He received them in radiance and joy. To the fear of the previous verse answers security: they feared, so they are preserved. naḍra — the same radiance as on the faces turned toward their Lord (s. 75, v. 22); surūr — inner joy. God turns their apprehension into light: it is the law of the heart, which reaps in peace what it sowed in fear.

mots.sens: from the evil of that day

mots.def: laqqā: to bring to meet, to grant.

mots.racine: ن ض ر / س ر ر

versets.ar: وَجَزَاهُم بِمَا صَبَرُوا جَنَّةً وَحَرِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-jazāhum bimā ṣabarū jannatan wa-ḥarīran
versets.trad: He rewarded them for their patience with a garden and garments of silk.
versets.commentaire: bimā ṣabarū (بِما صَبَروا): for what they endured. Qushayrī: “He rewarded them for the hunger they bore with a Garden and silk.” Patience (ṣabr) is the key word: they went hungry to feed others, they feared to guard themselves; all this is endurance. And endurance is the only coin exchanged for the Garden. What is given in hardship here is received in ease there.

mots.def: ṣabara: to be patient, to endure.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / ح ر ر

versets.ar: مُّتَّكِئِينَ فِيهَا عَلَى الْأَرَائِكِ لَا يَرَوْنَ فِيهَا شَمْسًا وَلَا زَمْهَرِيرًا
versets.tr: muttakiʾīna fīhā ʿalā l-arāʾiki lā yarawna fīhā shamsan wa-lā zamharīran
versets.trad: There, reclining on couches of honour, they will suffer neither burning sun nor biting cold.
versets.commentaire: lā shamsan wa-lā zamharīran (لا شَمْسًا وَلا زَمْهَريرًا): neither burning sun nor freezing cold. Qushayrī: “they suffer there from neither heat nor cold.” The Garden is the perfect equilibrium, away from the two excesses. An image of the soul at peace: neither the burning of desire (the sun) nor the ice of indifference (the cold), but the just mean, the tempered gentleness in which the heart at last rests, reclining.

versets.ar: وَدَانِيَةً عَلَيْهِمْ ظِلَالُهَا وَذُلِّلَتْ قُطُوفُهَا تَذْلِيلًا
versets.tr: wa-dāniyatan ʿalayhim ẓilāluhā wa-dhullilat quṭūfuhā tadhlīlan
versets.trad: Its shade will cover them, and its clusters of fruit will bow down for the picking.
versets.commentaire: dhullilat quṭūfuhā (ذُلِّلَتْ قُطوفُها): its fruits are made docile. Qushayrī: “they pick them in whatever position they are in, without effort; sitting, the clusters descend to them; standing, they rise up to them.” The fruit itself becomes a servant, bending toward the hand. This is the paradisal reversal: here below man tires himself to reach the fruit; there the fruit tires itself to come to man. Desire need no longer stretch out: it is anticipated.

versets.ar: وَيُطَافُ عَلَيْهِم بِآنِيَةٍ مِّن فِضَّةٍ وَأَكْوَابٍ كَانَتْ قَوَارِيرَا
versets.tr: wa-yuṭāfu ʿalayhim bi-āniyatin min fiḍḍatin wa-akwābin kānat qawārīra
versets.trad: Vessels of silver and goblets of crystal will be passed around among them,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes an essential remark here: “the name is silver, but the thing does not resemble the thing” (al-ism fiḍḍa, wa-l-ʿayn lā tushbihu l-ʿayn). In other words, Paradise is described with the words of our highest ideas of earthly beauty — silver, crystal — but the realities are of another order. The words are our words; the things they name surpass all we can conceive. The description is a ladder, not a measure.

versets.ar: قَوَارِيرَ مِن فِضَّةٍ قَدَّرُوهَا تَقْدِيرًا
versets.tr: qawārīra min fiḍḍatin qaddarūhā taqdīran
versets.trad: crystal of silver, filled to the exact measure.
versets.commentaire: qaddarūhā taqdīran (قَدَّروها تَقْديرًا): measured to the just measure. Qushayrī: “in the clarity of crystal and the whiteness of silver — this is measured according to the extent of their desire.” A wonder: the cup is made to the exact measure of the one who will drink, neither too much nor too little. In the Garden, the gift perfectly fits the will: each receives according to his capacity, and none overflows or falls short. Perfect measure is itself a sign of love.

versets.ar: وَيُسْقَوْنَ فِيهَا كَأْسًا كَانَ مِزَاجُهَا زَنجَبِيلًا
versets.tr: wa-yusqawna fīhā kaʾsan kāna mizājuhā zanjabīlan
versets.trad: There they will be given to drink a cup whose mixture is ginger,
versets.commentaire: zanjabīl (زَنْجَبيل): ginger. Qushayrī: “what is meant is the pleasant fragrance; the Arabs prized ginger, relished its aroma.” After the coolness of camphor (v. 5), here is the warmth of ginger: the drink of the righteous moves from soothed rest to rekindled ardour. Two states of the fulfilled heart: the peace that quenches, then the fervour that revives. The way is not a cold rest, but a living warmth.

versets.ar: عَيْنًا فِيهَا تُسَمَّىٰ سَلْسَبِيلًا
versets.tr: ʿaynan fīhā tusammā salsabīlan
versets.trad: drawn from a nearby spring called “Salsabīl.”
versets.commentaire: Salsabīl (سَلْسَبيل): the name of a spring; the word, as two words sal sabīl, can be heard as “seek the path.” Qushayrī: “they are given to drink from a spring — and the most perfect drinking is when it is the Real Himself who gives to drink, with no intermediary.” The spring's name is a hidden invitation: the spring of ginger encourages the pure to pursue their quest, a warm stage between the coolness of camphor and the unmixed water of Tasnīm. To drink, here, is still to advance.

mots.def: Salsabīl: name of a spring; “seek the path.”

versets.ar: وَيَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدَانٌ مُّخَلَّدُونَ إِذَا رَأَيْتَهُمْ حَسِبْتَهُمْ لُؤْلُؤًا مَّنثُورًا
versets.tr: wa-yaṭūfu ʿalayhim wildānun mukhalladūna idhā raʾaytahum ḥasibtahum luʾluʾan manthūran
versets.trad: Immortal youths will circulate among them. When you see them, you would take them for scattered pearls,
versets.commentaire: luʾluʾan manthūran (لُؤْلُؤًا مَنْثورًا): scattered pearls. Qushayrī: “when you see them, you take them, at the purity of their lustre, for scattered pearls.” They are compared to scattered pearls — and not, like the spouses, to pearls hidden in their shell — because they are lively and eager in service, coming and going. The image speaks of beauty that gives itself in movement: not the pearl kept, but the pearl offered.

mots.racine: ل أ ل أ / ن ث ر

versets.ar: وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ ثَمَّ رَأَيْتَ نَعِيمًا وَمُلْكًا كَبِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-idhā raʾayta thamma raʾayta naʿīman wa-mulkan kabīran
versets.trad: and when you look there, you will see bliss and a vast kingdom.
versets.commentaire: mulkan kabīran (مُلْكًا كَبيرًا): an immense kingdom. Qushayrī reports several glosses: that the angels ask their leave to enter; or “they have there all they desire”; or again: “a kingdom without decline.” The greatness of this kingdom lies not in its extent but in its permanence and its fullness: one has everything, and that everything does not pass. It is the exact opposite of the ʿājila, the hasty life that flees (s. 75, v. 20).

mots.racine: م ل ك / ك ب ر

versets.ar: عَالِيَهُمْ ثِيَابُ سُندُسٍ خُضْرٌ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٌ وَحُلُّوا أَسَاوِرَ مِن فِضَّةٍ وَسَقَاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ شَرَابًا طَهُورًا
versets.tr: ʿāliyahum thiyābu sundusin khuḍrun wa-istabraqun wa-ḥullū asāwira min fiḍḍatin wa-saqāhum rabbuhum sharāban ṭahūran
versets.trad: They will wear green garments of fine silk and brocade. They will be adorned with bracelets of silver. Their Lord will give them a pure drink to quench their thirst:
versets.commentaire: The summit-verse of the whole sūra, and the object of Qushayrī's longest meditation. sharāb ṭahūr (شَراب طَهور): a drink pure and purifying. Qushayrī: “the purifying drink is the one that is pure in itself and purifies what is other than itself.” And he spins out the wonder: this drink “purifies them of the love of all that is not Him (al-aghyār); whoever tastes the least sip of it is purified of the love of all creatures”; it “purifies the breasts of rancour and deceit,” and even “of the love of the houris.” Decisive: it is their Lord who gives them to drink — with no intermediary. Qushayrī then unfolds the degrees of this intoxication: the one who drinks the wine of love becomes generous to all, dust beneath the feet of the humblest, sometimes carried to the ecstasy that utters words of overflow (shaṭḥ) — thus Moses' “Show Yourself to me,” read as the intoxication of having heard the Word. And the wine of tawḥīd (التَّوحيد) effaces the witnessing of all other: the servant, at the beginning of the unveiling, is absorbed (mustawʿib), then engulfed (mustaghraq), then annihilated (mustahlak) — “and it is toward your Lord that the final end is.”

mots.racine: ث و ب / خ ض ر
ح ل ي / س و ر
mots.def: ḥullā: to be adorned; siwār: bracelet.

mots.racine: س ق ي / ر ب ب
mots.sens: and their Lord gave them to drink

mots.racine: ش ر ب / ط ه ر

versets.ar: إِنَّ هَٰذَا كَانَ لَكُمْ جَزَاءً وَكَانَ سَعْيُكُم مَّشْكُورًا
versets.tr: inna hādhā kāna lakum jazāʾan wa-kāna saʿyukum mashkūran
versets.trad: “This is granted to you as a reward. Your striving has been acknowledged!”
versets.commentaire: saʿyukum mashkūran (سَعْيُكُمْ مَشْكورًا): your striving accepted, acknowledged. Qushayrī: “His acknowledgement of their striving is that He multiplies the reward for the little work”; and higher: “His praise of them, by mentioning their goodness, in the way of honour.” A wonder of reversal: God thanks the servant. The one who expected “neither reward nor thanks” (v. 9) receives the thanks of God Himself. He who did not wish to be thanked is thanked by the King.

versets.ar: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ تَنزِيلًا
versets.tr: innā naḥnu nazzalnā ʿalayka l-qurʾāna tanzīlan
versets.trad: Truly, We have sent down upon you the Quran as a revelation.
versets.commentaire: nazzalnā… tanzīlan (نَزَّلْنا تَنْزيلًا): We sent it down by a graded descent. Qushayrī: “over the years.” The Quran did not fall all at once but unfolded in time, espousing circumstances and the growth of hearts. This patient descent is itself a mercy: the Word gives itself at the pace at which it can be received, as food adapts to the strength of the one who eats.

versets.ar: فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ وَلَا تُطِعْ مِنْهُمْ آثِمًا أَوْ كَفُورًا
versets.tr: fa-ṣbir li-ḥukmi rabbika wa-lā tuṭiʿ minhum āthiman aw kafūran
versets.trad: So patiently accept the decree of your Lord, and obey none of them who is a sinful ingrate or an obstinate denier.
versets.commentaire: fa-ṣbir li-ḥukmi rabbika (فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّك): be patient under the judgement of your Lord. Qushayrī opens here the whole doctrine of acceptance: “be satisfied with His decree (riḍā bi-qaḍāʾih), surrender to His judgement (istislām li-ḥukmih).” True patience is not gritting one's teeth but consenting from the heart. And its active reverse: do not obey the sinner or the ingrate — that is, “devote your obedience to your Lord alone.” Patient toward God, free toward men.

mots.racine: ح ك م / ر ب ب
mots.sens: for the decree of your Lord

mots.racine: أ ث م / ك ف ر

versets.ar: وَاذْكُرِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا
versets.tr: wa-dhkuri sma rabbika bukratan wa-aṣīlan
versets.trad: Invoke the Name of your Lord morning and evening.
versets.commentaire: wa-dhkuri sma rabbika (وَاذْكُرِ اسْمَ رَبِّك): invoke, recall the Name of your Lord, morning and evening. At the heart of patience and the refusal of evil, the central remedy: dhikr. Qushayrī ties to the verse the order of the prescribed prayers — dawn and decline marking the bounds of the day. But remembrance overflows the appointed hour: morning and evening, that is, without ceasing. What holds the heart in patience and preserves it from obeying evil is this repeated mention that keeps it turned toward its source.

mots.racine: س م و / ر ب ب
ب ك ر / أ ص ل
mots.def: bukra: morning; aṣīl: end of day.

versets.ar: وَمِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَاسْجُدْ لَهُ وَسَبِّحْهُ لَيْلًا طَوِيلًا
versets.tr: wa-mina l-layli fa-sjud lahu wa-sabbiḥhu laylan ṭawīlan
versets.trad: And at night, prostrate yourself before Him and glorify Him through the long hours.
versets.commentaire: wa-sabbiḥhu laylan ṭawīlan (وَسَبِّحْهُ لَيْلًا طَويلًا): glorify Him a long night. Qushayrī distinguishes the obligatory and the supererogatory: after the prescribed prayers comes the nāfila, the free vigil, a gift of love beyond duty. The long night is the time of the intimate: when the world sleeps, the heart keeps watch alone with its Lord. To prostrate and glorify in the darkness, with no witness, is the prayer of pure love, which shows itself to no one.

mots.sens: and a part of the night

mots.racine: ل ي ل / ط و ل

versets.ar: إِنَّ هَٰؤُلَاءِ يُحِبُّونَ الْعَاجِلَةَ وَيَذَرُونَ وَرَاءَهُمْ يَوْمًا ثَقِيلًا
versets.tr: inna hāʾulāʾi yuḥibbūna l-ʿājilata wa-yadharūna warāʾahum yawman thaqīlan
versets.trad: These people love the fleeting life and leave behind them a grave Day.
versets.commentaire: An exact echo of the previous sūra (75, v. 20): yuḥibbūna l-ʿājila — they love the immediate. Qushayrī: “they do not work for the Day of Resurrection.” Yawman thaqīlan: a heavy Day, weighted with consequences, which they cast behind them (warāʾahum) so as not to see it. The whole contrast of the sūra lies there: the righteous feared this Day (v. 10) and prepared for it; these thrust it at their backs. One carries before oneself what one loves, behind oneself what one flees.

mots.racine: ح ب ب / ع ج ل
و ذ ر / و ر ي

versets.ar: نَّحْنُ خَلَقْنَاهُمْ وَشَدَدْنَا أَسْرَهُمْ وَإِذَا شِئْنَا بَدَّلْنَا أَمْثَالَهُمْ تَبْدِيلًا
versets.tr: naḥnu khalaqnāhum wa-shadadnā asrahum wa-idhā shiʾnā baddalnā amthālahum tabdīlan
versets.trad: We created them and made firm their frame. Yet if We willed, We could replace them with others like them.
versets.commentaire: shadadnā asrahum (شَدَدْنا أَسْرَهُمْ): We strengthened their constitution, their joints. And the warning: baddalnā amthālahum — We could replace them with their likes. Qushayrī: “We would make them vanish and create others in their place.” A reminder of the utter gratuitousness of existence: no one is indispensable. The one who struts in the ʿājila forgets that he hangs by a thread; the Creator who knit him can unknit him and begin again. Humility is born of this acknowledged precariousness.

mots.sens: it is We who created them

mots.racine: ش د د / أ س ر
mots.def: asr: constitution, joint; shadda: to strengthen.

mots.racine: ب د ل / م ث ل

versets.ar: إِنَّ هَٰذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَن شَاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا
versets.tr: inna hādhihi tadhkiratun fa-man shāʾa ttakhadha ilā rabbihi sabīlan
versets.trad: This is truly a Reminder. Let whoever wills take a path toward his Lord;
versets.commentaire: tadhkira (تَذْكِرَة): a reminder — “the Quran is a reminder,” glosses Qushayrī. Not new information, but the awakening of a sleeping knowledge: man remembers what he already carried. And the door is wide open: fa-man shāʾa — “let whoever wills take a path toward his Lord.” The way is forced on no one; it is offered to whoever wills it. Willing is enough to open the path — a willing that will itself be illumined in the next verse.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / س ب ل

versets.ar: وَمَا تَشَاءُونَ إِلَّا أَن يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا
versets.tr: wa-mā tashāʾūna illā an yashāʾa llāhu inna llāha kāna ʿalīman ḥakīman
versets.trad: but you will not, unless God wills. God, indeed, knows all and is wise.
versets.commentaire: The verse that turns over the previous one and crowns it. “Let whoever wills…” — but you will not, unless God wills. The free choice offered in verse 29 is at once rooted in the divine Will: even your willing to take the path is a gift. Far from abolishing freedom, this verse unveils its source: man truly wills, but his willing is given to him. ʿalīm ḥakīm: God knows (the end of each) and acts with wisdom (in granting the willing where He knows). The mystery is not a constraint but an intimacy: your very desire for Him comes from Him.

mots.racine: ع ل م / ح ك م

versets.ar: يُدْخِلُ مَن يَشَاءُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ وَالظَّالِمِينَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا
versets.tr: yudkhilu man yashāʾu fī raḥmatihi wa-l-ẓālimīna aʿadda lahum ʿadhāban alīman
versets.trad: He admits whom He wills into His mercy. And for the wrongdoers He has prepared a painful chastisement.
versets.commentaire: A closing verse that seals the two slopes of the sūra. yudkhilu man yashāʾu fī raḥmatihi (يُدْخِلُ مَنْ يَشاءُ في رَحْمَتِه): He admits whom He wills into His mercy. Mercy is not a place one enters by one's own strength alone: one is brought in, welcomed by the One who opens the door. For the wrongdoers (al-ẓālimīn), “a painful chastisement” — Qushayrī: “whose pain penetrates to their very hearts.” To the righteous, given a pure drink by their Lord (v. 21), answer the unjust with the chastisement that bites the heart: two destinies parted by the orientation of love. Where love goes, the whole being goes.

mots.racine: ع د د / ع ذ ب / أ ل م
mots.sens: He has prepared a painful chastisement
mots.def: aʿadda: to prepare; ʿadhāb alīm: painful chastisement.
